interactions.label: Camera 2 Yaw (R)
interactions.teaches: Rotating Camera 2 updates its coordinate system and shifts the projected point p2 across its image plane according to the homography matrix H = K' R K^-1.

interactions.label: Point Depth (Z)
interactions.teaches: Changing the depth of the 3D point P slides it along the ray but leaves projected positions p1 and p2 completely unchanged, proving rotation homography is depth-independent.

interactions.label: View Perspective
interactions.teaches: Switching views highlights either the global 3D geometric layout or the exact 2D projections inside each camera's viewport.

demo_steps.title: Coincident Centers, Rotated Views
demo_steps.narration: Both cameras share the exact same optical center C, but Camera 2 is rotated relative to Camera 1. Because there is no translation between them, they view the scene from the same standpoint but through different orientations.

demo_steps.title: Depth Invariance Proof
demo_steps.narration: Slide the depth of point P. Notice that although P moves closer or further in 3D space, its projected points p1 and p2 do not move at all! Without translation, depth has no effect on the mapping between the two image planes.

demo_steps.title: Direct Homography Warp
demo_steps.narration: Since depth is irrelevant, a unique 2D-to-2D homography matrix H maps any point p1 on Image Plane 1 directly to its counterpart p2 on Image Plane 2. Rotating Camera 2 dynamically updates this transformation.

demo_steps.title: The Projection from Camera 1
demo_steps.narration: Switching to Camera 1's perspective, we see the grid of points projected onto its local 2D coordinate system. The homography mapping allows us to predict exactly where these points land in Camera 2.

plan.camera_suggestion: Elevated perspective view from the side-front, looking down at the shared camera center C at the origin, showing both image planes extending outwards with rays shooting towards the 3D point.

camera_view.view_notes: The shared camera center C sits at the origin. Image plane 1 is perpendicular to Z-axis. Image plane 2 rotates around Y. 3D point P is placed at positive Z and some X offset to make rays clearly split.

inline_constraints.default_view: must match standard projective geometry textbook figures with clean black coordinate lines and distinct colored image planes
inline_constraints.visible_ui: compact sliders and view toggles placed unobtrusively at the bottom or top-right edge
inline_constraints.explainers: tooltips on hover over p1, p2, and C
inline_constraints.framing: keep the shared origin C well within the visible bounds, ensuring frustum lines are not clipped
inline_constraints.camera: match the textbook elevation to make the rotation around the Y-axis clear

plan.notes: Render camera 1 plane in translucent blue and camera 2 plane in translucent red/orange. The coordinate axes for both frames should be color-coded (R, G, B for X, Y, Z respectively) to visually reinforce the rotation R between the two frames. When viewMode is not '3D Setup', smoothly transition the camera matrix to align with the selected camera's local coordinate frame.

elements: Origin point (projection center) represented as a black sphere at (0,0,0)
Camera 1 image plane (semi-transparent green rectangle) placed at z = f1 along Camera 1's optical axis
Camera 2 image plane (semi-transparent red rectangle) sharing the same origin, rotated around the vertical Y-axis by angle theta
Camera 1 local coordinate axes (X, Y, Z in green) at the origin
Camera 1 optical axis (dashed green line perpendicular to Camera 1 plane)
Camera 2 optical axis (dashed red line perpendicular to Camera 2 plane)
Curved rotation arrow R pointing from Camera 1's plane to Camera 2's plane
A 3D world point P (light blue sphere) floating in space at coordinates (X, Y, Z)
A dashed projection ray starting at the origin (0,0,0), passing through both image planes, and ending at point P
Projected point p = (x, y) on Camera 1 plane (small cyan sphere with black border)
Projected point p' = (x', y') on Camera 2 plane (small cyan sphere with black border)
Dashed perpendicular lines from the origin to each plane, with right-angle indicators matching the 2D diagram

interactions.label: Rotation R
interactions.teaches: Rotating Camera 2 shifts its image plane relative to Camera 1, altering the homography mapping between their corresponding projection points

interactions.label: Point Depth Z
interactions.teaches: Changing the depth of the 3D point does not alter its projection coordinates (x,y) or (x',y'), demonstrating that the homography is purely projective and independent of scene depth

demo_steps.title: Two cameras, one center
demo_steps.narration: Camera 1 (green) and Camera 2 (red) share the exact same projection center (the black sphere). Because there is no translation between them, any 3D ray from the origin intersects both image planes at unique, fixed locations.

demo_steps.title: Depth independence
demo_steps.narration: Slide the Point Depth. Notice that as the 3D point P moves closer or further along the ray, the projected points (x, y) and (x', y') stay perfectly stationary. Because the mapping between the two image planes depends only on the ray direction, the transformation is a homography independent of scene depth.

demo_steps.title: Rotating the camera
demo_steps.narration: Now rotate Camera 2. As the camera sweeps, the projected point (x', y') shifts across the red plane. The mathematical relationship between (x, y) and (x', y') is described by a 3x3 homography matrix H, determined solely by the rotation R and internal camera parameters.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: An elevated perspective showing both the 3D plane separations and the shared projection center clearly, matching the left-hand textbook illustration.

camera_view.view_notes: We look from the front-left-above to replicate the visual hierarchy of the 3D illustration. The green plane faces the viewer more directly, while the red plane is tilted away.

inline_constraints.default_view: Must closely match the visual styling, coordinate systems, and relative plane orientations of the textbook figure.
inline_constraints.visible_ui: Use at most two edge-placed sliders for Rotation and Depth to maintain an uncluttered visual field.
inline_constraints.explainers: Use dynamic labels for (x, y) and (x', y') directly on the planes that update reactively.
inline_constraints.framing: Reserve ample margins so the 3D scene elements do not feel clipped at typical aspect ratios.
inline_constraints.camera: Match the transition angle seamlessly so the interactive mode feels like a direct, high-fidelity expansion of the original diagram.

interactionPlan.notes: Represent the projection planes as DoubleSide transparent materials (opacity ~ 0.6) so intersections are highly legible from both sides. When animating the depth slider, the line segment beyond the planes should stretch while keeping the projection points locked in place on their respective meshes.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: Flipped orientation: The projection center (black sphere) is on the right in the generated figure, whereas it is on the left in both panels of the original figure.
The generated figure merges the 3D view (left panel) and the 2D schematic (right panel) into a single 3D scene, losing the clarity of the dual-panel textbook layout.
The right-angle indicators (perpendicular markers) are rendered as raw line segments that look like floating debris rather than clean, standard square symbols.
The camera labels are misplaced: 'Camera 1' is floating near the rotation arrow at the top right instead of pointing clearly to the green plane/system.
evaluation.notes: The generated figure mathematically implements the shared-center homography model with good interactive sliders, but its spatial layout is completely flipped compared to the original, and it attempts to merge two distinct textbook panels into one confusing scene.
action_items: Invert the camera/view geometry so the projection center is on the bottom-left and projects towards the right, matching the textbook's natural flow.
Split the canvas into two viewports or provide a toggle to switch between the 3D perspective view (left panel) and the clean 2D schematic projection view (right panel).
Refine the right-angle perpendicular indicators on the optical axes to be clean, recognizable square symbols.
Reposition the labels ('Camera 1', 'Camera 2') so they are clearly associated with their respective image planes/lines.

feedback.rationale: The plan failed to capture the dual-panel textbook structure, incorrectly merging 3D and 2D views into a single confusing scene, and got the spatial orientation flipped; a revised interaction and layout plan is required.
action_items: Invert the camera/view geometry so the projection center is on the bottom-left and projects towards the right, matching the textbook's natural flow.
Split the canvas into two viewports or provide a toggle to switch between the 3D perspective view (left panel) and the clean 2D schematic projection view (right panel).
Refine the right-angle perpendicular indicators on the optical axes to be clean, recognizable square symbols.
Reposition the labels ('Camera 1', 'Camera 2') so they are clearly associated with their respective image planes/lines.

elements: Origin point C (projection center) represented as a small neutral grey sphere at (0,0,0)
Camera 1 image plane: semi-transparent green rectangle placed perpendicular to the X-axis (Camera 1 optical axis) at x = 3
Camera 2 image plane: semi-transparent red rectangle of same dimensions, sharing the origin but rotated around the vertical Y-axis by angle theta
Camera 1 optical axis: dashed green line along the X-axis from origin through the center of Camera 1 plane
Camera 2 optical axis: dashed red line from origin through the center of Camera 2 plane
Right-angle perpendicular indicators: clean, recognizable square symbols in green/red at the intersection of each optical axis and its respective image plane
Label 'Camera 1' positioned floating just above the top edge of the green image plane
Label 'Camera 2' positioned floating just above the top edge of the red image plane, rotating dynamically with it
A 3D world point P (bright blue sphere) floating in space on a projection ray
Projection ray: thin dashed white line starting at the origin (0,0,0), passing through both image planes, and terminating at point P
Projected point p1: small green sphere at the intersection of the ray and Camera 1 plane
Projected point p2: small red sphere at the intersection of the ray and Camera 2 plane
Curved rotation arrow: arc on the horizontal XZ plane showing the angular sweep theta from the green axis to the red axis
2D Schematic elements: flat lines representing the 2D top-down projection of both planes (active in 2D Schematic mode)

interactions.label: Rotation θ
interactions.teaches: Rotating Camera 2 shifts its plane relative to Camera 1, modifying the 2D projective mapping (homography) between the corresponding coordinates p1 and p2.

interactions.label: Point Depth
interactions.teaches: Changing the depth of point P along the ray leaves the projection coordinates p1 and p2 completely unchanged, illustrating that a rotation-only homography is entirely independent of scene depth.

interactions.label: View Mode
interactions.teaches: Switches between a 3D perspective setup and a clean 2D top-down schematic view, highlighting the geometric relationships between the planes.

demo_steps.title: Shared center, distinct views
demo_steps.narration: Camera 1 (green) and Camera 2 (red) share the exact same projection center at the bottom-left origin. Since there is no translation between them, they view the world from the same point in space, but through different orientations.

demo_steps.title: Depth independence
demo_steps.narration: Vary the point depth along the ray. Notice that as the 3D point P moves closer or farther, its projected positions p1 and p2 remain perfectly static on both image planes. The mapping between them is a pure homography, unaffected by scene depth.

demo_steps.title: 2D schematic analysis
demo_steps.narration: Switch to the 2D Schematic view. Looking from directly above, we see the clean geometry of the intersecting image lines. The right-angle square symbols verify that each plane is perpendicular to its respective optical axis.

demo_steps.title: Dynamic camera rotation
demo_steps.narration: Rotate Camera 2 and watch the red plane sweep. The path of the intersection point p2 shifts continuously along the red line, governed strictly by the homography matrix H which depends only on the rotation θ and internal parameters.

plan.camera_suggestion: An orthographic view looking from front-left-above to position the camera origin C on the bottom-left, with the projection pointing towards the right, matching the textbook diagram's layout.

camera_view.view_notes: Origin is centered near the left edge of the screen, with optical axes extending to the right. In 2D Schematic mode, the camera transitions to an elevation of 90 degrees (top-down view) centered over the intersecting lines.

inline_constraints.default_view: Must match the original book illustration with the projection center on the left projecting towards the right.
inline_constraints.visible_ui: At most two compact sliders and one compact toggle near the right edge; no large overlay panels.
inline_constraints.explainers: Use dynamic tooltips on p1 and p2 showing their coordinate relationship.
inline_constraints.framing: Maintain ample side margins to accommodate the full swing of Camera 2 without clipping.
inline_constraints.camera: The initial render state must perfectly align with the viewpoint of the 3D book figure.

plan.notes: When 'viewMode' is set to '2D Schematic', smoothly animate the camera to elevation 90, azimuth -90. Ensure the right-angle symbols are rendered as clean thin-line 3D square segments aligned to the respective camera coordinate axes.
